text: diversification, tillage methods are statistically significant at 5% significance level. While agroforestry, drought-resistant varieties, and irrigation are statistically significant at 5% significance level. We take turns to discuss the adaptation strategies in the ensuing paragraphs. Our results show that adopting climate variability adaptation strategies such as agricultural diversification, appropriate tillage methods, and irrigation positively influences maize yield. This means that smallholders use of these adaptation strategies positively affects maize yields. On the other hand, agroforestry practices and planting drought-resistant varieties as a climate variability adaptation strategy had an inverse relationship on the maize yield obtained in the study area. Other climate variability adaptation strategies (fertilizer application, shifting culti vation, pests and disease control etc.) were not statistically significant, implying that the adoption of such climate variability adaptation strategies does not significantly affect maize yield in the Cape Coast Metropolitan Assembly, holding all other things equal. Ankrah et al., Cogent Food & Agriculture (2023), 9: 2247166 https://doi.org/10.1080/23311932.2023.2247166 Page 10 of 23 Agricultural diversification can be defined as increasing the portfolio of crops that a farmer cultivates, thus a shift from monoculture, towards multiple crops and livestocks with the intention to meet competitive market demands and improving upon farm profits in order to reduce vulner ability from losses from just a single farm enterprise (Asante et al., 2018 ; Bellon et al., 2020 ). Agricultural diversification had a positive relationship with the quantity of maize produced. Agricultural diversification has been widely recommended as a means of avoiding total crop loss in the event of a disaster. Indeed, Dumenu and Obeng ( 2016 ) indicated that agricultural diversi fication constituted a prominent climate variability adaptation strategy employed by smallholder farmers in Ghana. Crop diversification or livestock diversification helps

text: Dumenu and Obeng ( 2016 ) indicated that agricultural diversi fication constituted a prominent climate variability adaptation strategy employed by smallholder farmers in Ghana. Crop diversification or livestock diversification helps mitigate the shocks of climate variability impacts. Mixed cropping is a common practice among farmers, often farmers cultivate maize, cassava, plantain, cowpeas, and other vegetables in their farming practices. Mixed farming which includes the rearing of livestock and the cultivation of crops (Low et al., 2023 ; Mekuria & Mekonnen, 2018 ). Generally mixed farming provides a good incentive for minimizing climate variability effects on agricultural households. Kosoe and Ahmed ( 2022 ) supported the finding that crop diversification improves crop yield among cash crop farmers. Diversification increases income generation and hence leads to improvements in farmers’ livelihood outcomes. Akinnagbe and Irohibe ( 2014 ) indicated that crop diversification is insurance against rainfall variability. On the contrary, Kumodzie ( 2018 ), found an inverse relationship between crop diversi fication and rice output in the Hohoe Municipality of Ghana. Our results, however showed that, agricultural diversification is a useful climate variability

title: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality Ghana (p. 12)
source: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality Ghana
text: ), found an inverse relationship between crop diversi fication and rice output in the Hohoe Municipality of Ghana. Our results, however showed that, agricultural diversification is a useful climate variability adaptation strategy that positively influ ences maize yield. Indeed, Baffour-Ata et al. ( 2021 ) recommended agricultural diversification as a useful climate variability adaptation strategy based on their study in Ghana (See Table 3 ). Agroforestry practice had a negative association with maize yield. This implies that adopting agroforestry as an adaptation strategy had negative returns on maize yield. Intercropping maize with the planting of trees becomes non-beneficial when the tree form canopies, which reduce sunlight intensity required for crop development, and competition for soil nutrients which potentially leads to low crop yields for maize (Gao et al., 2013 ; Raza et al., 2020 ). For instance, tree planting may reduce the space available for maize cultivation and therefore, can decrease maize yield relative to when all the land is dedicated to the cultivation of maize. Food crops tend to perform better when intercrop with other food crops and not

doc_id: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality  Ghana__0041
title: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality Ghana (p. 12)
source: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality Ghana
text: decrease maize yield relative to when all the land is dedicated to the cultivation of maize. Food crops tend to perform better when intercrop with other food crops and not trees. For instance, the intercropping of maize and legumes tend to enrich the soil and translate into higher maize yields. In other words, farmers who use their farms mainly to cultivate maize (monocropping) may have the opportunity to experience an increase in maize yield. This is because farm inputs such as fertilizer, disease and pest control are dedicated solely for maize production. Contrary to this assertion, Akinnagbe and Irohibe ( 2014 ) reported that agroforestry practices increase organic matter in the soil, which serves as a source of soil nutrients and increases crop productivity. Indeed, there are tree crops that help fix nitrogen in the soil and help improve soil fertility. Tree crops can generally be intercropped with food crops, including maize, when the canopy is not properly formed and closed. This happens typically during the formative years (1–5 years). It is important to note that our study site falls

doc_id: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality  Ghana__0042
title: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality Ghana (p. 12)
source: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality Ghana
text: including maize, when the canopy is not properly formed and closed. This happens typically during the formative years (1–5 years). It is important to note that our study site falls within the coastal area, hence the agroecology does not support tree crop production. Our study provides novel evidence to the already existing evidence on how agroforestry inversely affects maize yields in the global south (See Table 4 ). Tillage methods positively influence maize yield in the study area. The tillage methods used in the study site are manual labour-intensive methods. This includes weeding with a hoe and cutlass, sowing maize with cutlass in making holes in the soil and covering with the hands or feet. Tillage methods among smallholder farmers often tend to be manual and less sophisticated. The cost involved in hiring tractors to plough, harrow, and till the farmlands appear expensive for small holder farmers. Fortunately for smallholder farmers in southern Ghana, their farmlands do not require ploughing and harrowing before planting. This contrasts with northern Ghana, where most farmers require ploughing and harrowing of the farmlands before

title: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality Ghana (pp. 12-13)
source: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality Ghana
text: farmers in southern Ghana, their farmlands do not require ploughing and harrowing before planting. This contrasts with northern Ghana, where most farmers require ploughing and harrowing of the farmlands before planting given the nature of their lands. Medium and commercial farmers however employ mechanization on their farms. Our result varies with Liu et al. ( 2020 ), who reported that no-tillage negatively affects or reduces crop yield. The study site is not an arid site that requires planting drought-resistant varieties to address challenges associated with droughts. Therefore, smallholder farmers who plant drought-resistant varieties do not get positive returns. This implies that it is not worthwhile to invest money into the purchase and planting of drought-resistant maize varieties. Even though drought stress can lead to yield losses of between 10–25% in drought-prone areas (Fisher et al., 2015 ). Indeed, Fisher et al. Our finding confirms other findings (Araya et al., 2017 ; Burke & Lobell, 2010 ; Fisher et al., 2015 ; Moradi et al., 2013 ) that indicated that irrigation as a climate variability adaptation strategy increases yields and profit margins, with an additional advantage of allowing flexibility in planting dates and the maize varieties that can be planted. Irrigation is a very common adaptation strategy, mainly in the arid regions of SSA. For instance, irrigation is very common in Burkina Faso where irrigation canals have been constructed since the 1970s (Fisher et al., 2015 ). Indeed, the related studies (Adams et al., 2021 ; Anang et al., 2020 ; Quaye et al., 2022 ) show that the sex of farmers affects the adoption of maize technologies in Ghana. Therefore, delayed rains imply sowing late, whereas and early rains, imply sowing early. Our result supports the finding of Tachie-Obeng et al. ( 2013 ), who found that early or delayed planting improved maize yields. The literature (Kassie et al., 2015 ; Shrestha, 2014 ) shows that delayed sowing prevents the crops from drought stress and heat during the early formative stages. Lv et al. ( 2020 ) and Saddique et al. ( 2020 ) supported the finding that delaying planting dates positively correlated with crop yields. The adjustment of planting date appears well documented in the related literature, but with sparing evidence in the climate variability and maize yields nexus literature. Our findings, therefore, improve understanding on the climate variability adaptation literature in Ghana, particularly for maize which is a staple food crop. As discussed earlier under the multiple regression analysis, we observe that few farmers practice irrigation as an adaptation strategy, however, among the few who do, they resort to simple irrigation techniques such as storage of water in barrels, planting in marshy areas, or on riverbanks, harvesting rain water into containers and using for irrigation in complementing the natural rains. This helps to improve farmers resilience to climate variability. title: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality Ghana (p. 17)
source: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality Ghana
text: climate variability adaptation strategy in non-arid regions (See Table 5 ). 4. Conclusion and policy recommendations Climate variabilities remain pervasive in the global world. However, farmers in sub-Saharan Africa (SSA) are disproportionately affected. Maize is an essential staple in SSA, and its production is mainly contingent on the onset of natural rainfall, which by default introduces uncertainties in production. The smallholder farmer’s ability to adapt to the adverse effects of climate variability is not in doubt, but the accounts on adaptation strategies that remain effective, has been largely anecdotal. Therefore, this study assessed the effect of adopting climate variability strategies (CVAS) on maize farmers yield in southern Ghana’s Cape Coast Metropolitan Assembly. The findings showed that some climate variability adaptation strategies (irrigation and early or late planting) positively correlated with maize yields. Irrigation positively correlated with maize yields, implying that adopting irrigation increases maize yields. We find that even though irrigation appears well established as a climate variability strategy in arid regions, the use of irrigation in non-arid regions among maize farmers is also gaining traction. Additionally, we found that

doc_id: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality  Ghana__0055
title: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality Ghana (p. 17)
source: Effect of climate variability adaptation strategies on maize yield in the Cape Coast Municipality Ghana
text: irrigation appears well established as a climate variability strategy in arid regions, the use of irrigation in non-arid regions among maize farmers is also gaining traction. Additionally, we found that changes in planting dates (early or late planting) positively affected maize yields implying that sustained agricultural extension and advisory services that target changes in planting dates can go a long way to enhance maize yields in southern Ghana. Our findings showed that borrowing funds, planting in rows, applying indigenous knowledge, shifting cultivation, agroforestry, and agricultural diversification, among others, do not statistically influence maize yields. The Ghana Irrigation Development Authority (GIDA) under the Ministry of Food and Agriculture (MoFA) is encouraged to institutionalize the informal irrigation schemes practiced by farmers to ensure all year production of maize. Perhaps, this is an opportunity to strengthen the “one village one dam” (1V1D) initiative of the government of Ghana to cover non- arid areas such as southern Ghana to increase maize production. Agricultural extension and advisory services are encouraged to promote and sustain increased interest in the changes to planting dates (early or late

Agricultural extension and advisory services are encouraged to promote and sustain increased interest in the changes to planting dates (early or late planting) as a useful climate variability adaptation strategy in mini mizing the adverse effects of climate variability. A scoping review of literature on climate change impacts among smallholder maize producers in low- and medium-income countries Samson Gachathi * and Daniel Muasya Nzengya School of Education and Social Sciences, St Paul’s University, Limuru, Kenya Climate change is redefining global agriculture through temperature swings, erratic rainfall, and frequent extreme events. According to the IPCC agricultural productivity growth in Sub-Saharan Africa has declined by approximately 5–6% due to climate warming, with maize yields particularly affected by temperatures exceeding 30–35 °C during critical growth stages. Smallholder farming com munities face disproportionate vulnerability to intensifying climate hazards, yet knowledge regarding specific mechanisms through which climate variability affects smallholder maize producers—and evidence on adaptation strategy effectiveness—remains fragmented and methodologically limited. This is particularly concerning in Sub-Saharan Africa, where over 70% of maize producers experi ence high exposure and sensitivity alongside low adaptive capacity. This scop ing review assessed gaps in empirical research on climate change impacts and adaptation responses among smallholder maize producers in LMICs. Following PRISMA guidelines, a systematic

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 1)
text: low adaptive capacity. This scop ing review assessed gaps in empirical research on climate change impacts and adaptation responses among smallholder maize producers in LMICs. Following PRISMA guidelines, a systematic search identified 86 peer-reviewed articles published between 2008 and 2025 and indexed in Scopus, Web of Science, and AGRICOLA. After removing duplicates using Zotero and excluding qualitativeonly studies, grey literature, studies with undefined outcome variables, and those without inferential statistics, 38 empirical peer reviewed articles employing quantitative or mixed method were analyzed using descriptive statistics in Microsoft Excel. The analysis revealed three main outcome variables: adaptation strategies (70.3%, n = 26), perception (21.6%, n = 8), and climate awareness (8.1%, n = 3). Geographically, studies concentrated heavily in Ethiopia (19%), Ghana (19%), and Zimbabwe (14%), while South Africa, Lesotho, Nigeria, Tanzania, Botswana, Burkina Faso, Cameroon, DRC, Eswatini, and Kenya each accounted for less than 10%, revealing clear research bias. Cross-sectional research design dominated (n = 32, 86%), followed by exploratory design ( n = 4, 11%) and longitudinal design (n = 1, 3%). Regarding theoretical frameworks, 28% of studies lacked

title: fclim 8 1704769 (pp. 1-2)
text: research design dominated (n = 32, 86%), followed by exploratory design ( n = 4, 11%) and longitudinal design (n = 1, 3%). Regarding theoretical frameworks, 28% of studies lacked any framework, 20% employed random utility maximization theory, and 52% drew on 13 other theories including adaptive capability, expected utility, protection motivation, socioecological system, and vulnerability theory— each accounting for 4%. Notably, no study incorporated moderation variables. The review reveals that while adaptation behaviors are well-documented, empirical evidence quantifying actual climate impacts on productivity, income, and awareness remains scarce. Future research should prioritize impact studies evaluating scalable interventions and utilize robust resilience metrics to strengthen smallholder maize systems. Gachathi and Nzengya 10.3389/fclim.2026.1704769 Frontiers in Climate 02 frontiersin.org KEYWORDS Climate adaptation strategies, climate change, Climate impact, maize producers, resilience, smallholder farmers, sustainability 1 Introduction and background Maize is one of the world’s most critical staple crops, sustaining food security and livelihoods for millions of smallholder households, especially in Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA), where production systems are predominantly rainfed and resource-constrained (Fan and Rue, 2020; Shiferaw et al., 2011). Since the 1960s, global

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 2)
text: for millions of smallholder households, especially in Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA), where production systems are predominantly rainfed and resource-constrained (Fan and Rue, 2020; Shiferaw et al., 2011). Since the 1960s, global maize area has nearly doubled to approximately 202 million hectares by 2020, while average yields have roughly tripled from 2 to 5.8 tonnes per hectare (Erenstein et al., 2022). Maize now contributes 25–50% of total calories in many developing regions, with over 30% of dietary calories in parts of Africa and Latin America derived from this crop (Lowder et al., 2021). Despite growing recognition of climate threats to maize systems, the empirical literature remains skewed toward documenting adaptation choices rather than rigorously quantifying measured climate impacts and resilience outcomes ( Mulungu and Ng’ ombe, 2019; Mnukwa et al., 2025). This imbalance matters because smallholder maize producers operate under structural constraints—limited access to irrigation, credit, extension, and soil fertility inputs—that shape what is feasible to adopt, how costly it is to adapt, and whether adaptation actually translates into stable yields, food security, and income gains (Hassan and Nhemachena, 2008; Kori, 2023;

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 2)
text: what is feasible to adopt, how costly it is to adapt, and whether adaptation actually translates into stable yields, food security, and income gains (Hassan and Nhemachena, 2008; Kori, 2023; Dimkpa et al., 2023). Consequently, although climate risks to smallholder maize systems are widely acknowledged, the empirical literature still offers insufficient, uneven, and methodologically limited evidence on what climate change is doing to productivity and livelihoods, which adaptation strategies measurably work, and under what contexts and constraints they succeed or fail (Mnukwa et al., 2025; Muoni et al., 2025). 1.1 Global impact of climate change on smallholder maize farmers Maize supports the food security and livelihoods of hundreds of millions of smallholder farmers, particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America, and parts of Asia (Lowder et al., 2021; Ricciardi et al., 2018). Despite recent record harvests, maize systems face mounting threats as climates warm. Rising temperatures, shifting rainfall patterns, and more frequent extreme weather events—including heat waves, droughts, and floods—increasingly undermine maize productivity and stability for smallholders worldwide (IPCC, 2022a, 2022b; Wheeler and von Braun, 2013). 1.1.1 Climate threats to maize

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 2)
text: frequent extreme weather events—including heat waves, droughts, and floods—increasingly undermine maize productivity and stability for smallholders worldwide (IPCC, 2022a, 2022b; Wheeler and von Braun, 2013). 1.1.1 Climate threats to maize production Maize production is increasingly exposed to a range of climaterelated stresses that directly undermine yields and farmer resilience. These threats manifest through multiple pathways, from rising temperatures and shifting rainfall patterns to escalating pest pressures and soil degradation. The following key challenges highlight the most critical climate risks confronting smallholder maize systems worldwide: • Temperature stress poses a critical challenge. Many maize-growing areas, especially in the tropics and subtropics, are experiencing hotter growing seasons. Maize is particularly sensitive to high temperatures during pollination, with yield losses occurring when daytime highs exceed 30–35 °C (De Groote et al., 2020; Ranum et al., 2021). Climate models project that each 1 °C of warming will reduce tropical maize yields by approximately 5% on average (Karwani G. M. et al., 2024; Karwani M. et al., 2024; Nelson et al., 2022). For example, IPCC reports indicate that maize yields in West Africa are expected

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 2)
text: on average (Karwani G. M. et al., 2024; Karwani M. et al., 2024; Nelson et al., 2022). For example, IPCC reports indicate that maize yields in West Africa are expected to decline by approximately 9% at 1.5 °C warming and by 41% at 4 °C relative to 2005 levels if no new adaptations occur (Kapari et al., 2023). Recent analyses project up to 40% declines in U. S. maize yields by late century under highemissions scenarios, with similar losses projected in China and parts of southern Africa ( Rodríguez-Barillas et al., 2024 ; Strandt, 2025). • Water stress and rainfall variability present equally severe challenges. Approximately 70–95% of global maize is rainfed (Nelson et al., 2022), making changes in precipitation patterns critical. In many smallholder regions, rainfall is becoming more erratic and drought frequency is rising. Periods of late-season drought are particularly damaging to maize, reducing kernel number and grain fill, while intensifying monsoons and heavy rainfall can cause waterlogging or soil erosion ( Tchouandem Nzali et al., 2024). Empirical studies confirm that agricultural productivity growth in Sub-Saharan Africa has

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 2)
text: fill, while intensifying monsoons and heavy rainfall can cause waterlogging or soil erosion ( Tchouandem Nzali et al., 2024). Empirical studies confirm that agricultural productivity growth in Sub-Saharan Africa has already been slowed by climate change, with maize yield growth approximately 5–6% lower than it would have been without warming (IPCC, 2022a, 2022b). • Pests, diseases, and soil degradation compound these challenges. Warming climates are expanding the range and seasonality of pests such as fall armyworm and stem borers, and pathogens like maize lethal necrosis. In Sub-Saharan Africa, pests and diseases already contribute 10–35% yield losses in maize (Lamichhane et al., 2022). Higher temperatures and humidity also accelerate soil degradation processes, reducing fertility on already marginal smallholder plots. Some models estimate up to a 50% increase in pest-related maize losses by mid-century (Lamichhane et al., 2022; Utobo and Asogwa, 2021). 1.1.2 Projected global impacts and smallholder vulnerability Climate change modeling consistently projects net negative impacts on maize productivity globally. A comprehensive analysis accounting for real farmer adaptations estimates that by 2,100, global maize yields will be 13–28% below what they

title: fclim 8 1704769 (pp. 2-3)
text: consistently projects net negative impacts on maize productivity globally. A comprehensive analysis accounting for real farmer adaptations estimates that by 2,100, global maize yields will be 13–28% below what they would be without climate change, with a median decline of 28% under RCP8.5 (Manzvera et al., 2024 ). Mid-21st century projections show most of North America, China, southern Africa, and parts of Europe suffering yield declines often exceeding 20–40% (Strandt, 2025). These climate risks interact disproportionately with longstanding smallholder constraints. Smallholder maize farmers often cultivate marginal rainfed lands with limited irrigation, use few inputs, and have minimal access to credit or high-yielding varieties. Consequently, their baseline yields and resilience are lower than those of commercial farms. While global average maize yields are approximately 5.8 tonnes Gachathi and Nzengya 10.3389/fclim.2026.1704769 Frontiers in Climate 03 frontiersin.org per hectare (De Groote et al., 2020), many African smallholder plots yield only 1–2 tonnes per hectare. The IPCC emphasizes that communities with climate-sensitive livelihoods are disproportionately vulnerable when climate hazards intensify, with vulnerability amplified by poverty and limited infrastructure (IPCC, 2022a, 2022b). 1.1.3 Global climate

text: hectare. The IPCC emphasizes that communities with climate-sensitive livelihoods are disproportionately vulnerable when climate hazards intensify, with vulnerability amplified by poverty and limited infrastructure (IPCC, 2022a, 2022b). 1.1.3 Global climate policy frameworks Over the past decade, international climate policy has increasingly recognized agriculture as a critical adaptation sector. The Paris Agreement (2015) explicitly calls for enhancing adaptive capacity in vulnerable sectors (Article 7), and subsequent COP decisions have sought to integrate agriculture into global climate strategies. Major institutions now emphasize climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and resilience-building. The World Bank’s Climate Change Action Plan (2021–2025) identifies agri-food systems as a key transition for meeting Paris targets and has scaled up climate-smart agriculture financing eightfold to approximately $3 billion per year since Paris (World Bank, 2025). However, current climate finance is heavily skewed: agrifood systems cause approximately 30% of global emissions but receive only 4% of climate funds, and just 20% of that reaches smallholders (Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, International Fund for Agricultural Development, UNICEF , World Food Programme, and World Health Organization, 2023). 1.2 Impact of climate change

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 3)
text: reaches smallholders (Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, International Fund for Agricultural Development, UNICEF , World Food Programme, and World Health Organization, 2023). 1.2 Impact of climate change on smallholder maize farmers in Sub-Saharan Africa Maize is the dominant staple crop across Sub-Saharan Africa, occupying approximately 41 million hectares—roughly 40% of the continent’s cereal area—with production rising from 67 to 91 million tons between 2010 and 2020 ( Erenstein et al., 2022 ). Despite this expansion, average SSA maize yields remain critically low at approximately 2 tonnes per hectare, representing only 30–50% of achievable potential and lagging significantly behind global yields. Smallholder farmers, who cultivate roughly 80% of SSA ’s maize on predominantly rainfed systems (95% of African farms lack irrigation), are particularly vulnerable to climate variability (Erenstein et al., 2022). Across SubSaharan Africa, 71% of smallholder households experience climate shocks, 54% endure at least one food-insecure month each year, and 80% lack the resources to adapt. Notably, over 70% of these maize producers face high exposure and sensitivity alongside low adaptive capacity, underscoring the urgent need for

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 3)
text: each year, and 80% lack the resources to adapt. Notably, over 70% of these maize producers face high exposure and sensitivity alongside low adaptive capacity, underscoring the urgent need for targeted, context-specific interventions (Ogunyiola et al., 2022). 1.2.1 Regional climate impacts Climate change does not affect maize production uniformly across Sub-Saharan Africa; instead, its impacts vary widely by region, reflecting differences in agroecological conditions, rainfall patterns, and exposure to extreme events. Understanding these regional dynamics is essential, as they reveal both the diversity of challenges smallholder farmers face and the need for context-specific adaptation strategies. The following highlights illustrate how climate variability manifests across Southern, Western, and Eastern Africa: • Southern Africa faces acute drought risk linked to El Niño climate oscillations. The 2023/24 El Niño event brought extreme dryness to Zambia, Malawi, and Zimbabwe, with February–March 2024 ranking among the driest months on record in many maize-growing areas. South African maize yield projections fell approximately 17% below normal levels (Nelson et al., 2022). Climate models project that above 1.5 °C of warming, drought frequency and duration will rise across

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 3)
text: African maize yield projections fell approximately 17% below normal levels (Nelson et al., 2022). Climate models project that above 1.5 °C of warming, drought frequency and duration will rise across much of Southern Africa, with critical precipitation declines in core maize areas (African Union, 2022; Nelson et al., 2022). • Western Africa’s maize belt—encompassing Nigeria, Ghana, Burkina Faso, Mali, and surrounding countries—has experienced pronounced warming trends and erratic rainfall patterns. Average maize yields in West Africa remain extremely low at approxi mately 1.2–1.3 tonnes per hectare (Erenstein et al., 2022; Sadiq et al., 2019). Extended dry spells diminish planting windows, while episodes of waterlogging occur during unusually heavy rains. High temperatures, often exceeding 30–35 °C during the growing season, further stress maize at flowering. The rapid spread of fall armyworm and invasive grain borers produces large year-to-year swings in national output, with yield losses ranging from 20–40% in infested areas (Njeru et al., 2023; Ogunyiola et al., 2022). • Eastern Africa exhibits diverse climates—from the Ethiopian highlands and Tanzanian midlands to Uganda’s lake basin—all facing mounting climate stress. Countries such

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 3)
text: (Njeru et al., 2023; Ogunyiola et al., 2022). • Eastern Africa exhibits diverse climates—from the Ethiopian highlands and Tanzanian midlands to Uganda’s lake basin—all facing mounting climate stress. Countries such as Ethiopia and Tanzania have experienced rapid expansion of maize cultivation area yet yields remain well below potential; Ethiopia’s maize yields average under 4.5 tonnes per hectare despite favorable highland conditions (Erenstein et al., 2022). The Horn of Africa endured its worst drought in 40 years in 2021, devastating harvests across Ethiopia, Somalia, and adjacent areas ( Karwani G. M. et al., 2024; Karwani M. et al., 2024; Nelson et al., 2022). Eastern African smallholders face a convergence of drought risk, heat stress, and pest outbreaks that produce increasingly unpredictable harvests. 1.2.2 Climate adaptation strategies and policy frameworks In response to these challenges, smallholder maize farmers employ diverse adaptation strategies. Common responses include drought-tolerant or early-maturing varieties and planting-date adjustments to match shortened rainy seasons (Atinkut and Mebrat, 2016; Bedeke et al., 2019). Farmers also diversify cropping systems through intercropping and agroforestry to spread risk and improve soil fertility (Giller

title: fclim 8 1704769 (pp. 3-4)
text: to match shortened rainy seasons (Atinkut and Mebrat, 2016; Bedeke et al., 2019). Farmers also diversify cropping systems through intercropping and agroforestry to spread risk and improve soil fertility (Giller et al., 2021; Gugissa et al., 2022). Soil and water conservation practices—minimum tillage, mulching, integrated nutrient management, and water harvesting—are used to conserve moisture and protect soil health (Dimkpa et al., 2023; Bedeke et al., 2018). Evidence shows that adaptation can affect performance outcomes such as technical efficiency and productivity, yet effects vary by setting and strategy (Adzawla and Alhassan, 2021; Kamuzora, 2023). At the continental level, African Union policy frameworks explicitly recognize climate threats to maize systems. Agenda 2063 prioritizes climate-resilient economies (African Union, 2022), while the 2014 Malabo Declaration reaffirmed commitments to transform agriculture by doubling productivity through sustainable practices (African Union Commission, 2015a, 2015b). However, implementation has lagged, with many countries’ National Agriculture Investment Plans initially failing to adequately address climate adaptation. The African Union's, 2022–2032 Climate Change and Resilient Gachathi and Nzengya 10.3389/fclim.2026.1704769 Frontiers in Climate 04 frontiersin.org Development Strategy recommend integrating climate adaptation measures within

text: to adequately address climate adaptation. The African Union's, 2022–2032 Climate Change and Resilient Gachathi and Nzengya 10.3389/fclim.2026.1704769 Frontiers in Climate 04 frontiersin.org Development Strategy recommend integrating climate adaptation measures within staple crop systems (African Union, 2022). 1.2.3 Evidence gaps and research priorities A major limitation of the current evidence base is its uneven distribution. Maize-focused climate adaptation studies are concentrated in Ethiopia and Ghana, with fewer rigorous empirical studies in other highly vulnerable contexts (Bedeke et al., 2019; Aidoo et al., 2021). Evidence from semi-arid Zimbabwe emphasizes how climate vulnerability and structural drivers—inputs, soil constraints, and farming conditions—intersect to sustain low yields, reinforcing the need to analyze climate impacts together with underlying agronomic and institutional constraints (Muoni et al., 2025; Dimkpa et al., 2023). The field needs stronger evidence on the magnitude of climate impacts, the effectiveness of specific adaptation bundles, and the distribution of benefits and burdens across different types of smallholder households (Abate et al., 2017; Olagunju et al., 2020; Kariuki, 2018). 1.3 Impact of climate change on smallholder maize farmers in Kenya Smallholder maize farming remains the

text: types of smallholder households (Abate et al., 2017; Olagunju et al., 2020; Kariuki, 2018). 1.3 Impact of climate change on smallholder maize farmers in Kenya Smallholder maize farming remains the backbone of Kenya’s food security and rural economy, employing millions, with the agriculture sector contributing approximately 22.5% of GDP [Kenya National Bureau of Statistics (KNBS), 2025]. Despite its critical importance, the sector faces escalating climate-driven challenges that threaten productivity, incomes, and livelihoods. Over recent decades, Kenya has experienced increased frequency and severity of droughts, erratic rainfall patterns, and rising temperatures—factors that compound the vulnerability of maize-dependent smallholders (Kabara et al., 2022). Evidence from major production areas, including Trans Nzoia, Uasin Gishu, and Bungoma, reveals yield declines of 15–25% over the past decade due to prolonged dry spells and unpredictable rainfall (Njeru et al., 2023). Temperature increases of just 1 °C above longterm averages have shortened maize growth periods, reducing grain yields by up to 20% (Njeru et al., 2023). The Rift Valley region, which contributes nearly half of national maize production, exemplifies how central smallholders are to the national food

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 4)
text: yields by up to 20% (Njeru et al., 2023). The Rift Valley region, which contributes nearly half of national maize production, exemplifies how central smallholders are to the national food supply (StatsKenya, 2025). Beyond climatic stressors, biological threats intensify production challenges. Warmer, more humid conditions have expanded the distribution of pests and diseases—particularly fall armyworm and maize streak virus—resulting in yield losses ranging from 20 to 40% in affected areas ( De Groote et al., 2020 ; Ogunyiola et al., 2022 ). Simultaneously, soil erosion, nutrient depletion, and declining organic matter degrade already fragile smallholder plots, diminishing their capacity to withstand environmental shocks (Bryan et al., 2022). Although national efforts promote drought-tolerant maize varieties, conservation agriculture, and agroforestry, adoption remains uneven and low—especially among women and resource-poor farmers—due to financial, informational, and institutional barriers (Atube et al., 2021). High costs for improved inputs, limited access to credit, under-resourced extension services, and insecure land tenure continue to constrain the use of climate-resilient technologies across Kenya’s rural counties (Wanjira et al., 2022). These constraints expose smallholders to recurrent yield losses and income

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 4)
text: and insecure land tenure continue to constrain the use of climate-resilient technologies across Kenya’s rural counties (Wanjira et al., 2022). These constraints expose smallholders to recurrent yield losses and income instability, forcing many into cycles of debt, asset loss, and reduced farm investment. Despite considerable global and regional research on climate change impacts in agriculture, significant knowledge gaps remain regarding the specific vulnerabilities of smallholder maize farmers in Kenya across diverse agroecological conditions and socioeconomic contexts. Existing research often aggregates findings across regions, masking the distinctly different vulnerabilities of smallholders in high-potential areas such as Trans Nzoia and Uasin Gishu compared to those in semi-arid counties like Machakos and Makueni. This lack of localized, context-specific evidence limits targeted climate adaptation planning and effective policy formulation. Furthermore, most literature focuses on either specific climatic impacts or adaptation practices in isolation, without integrating the structural and institutional dimensions that shape farmers’ responses. Evidence remains limited on how gender, credit access, and extension services influence adaptive decisions at the individual smallholder level. Consequently, existing policies and programs often adopt blanket approaches unable to

text: Evidence remains limited on how gender, credit access, and extension services influence adaptive decisions at the individual smallholder level. Consequently, existing policies and programs often adopt blanket approaches unable to capture the localized realities of smallholders facing different climatic and socioeconomic challenges. Addressing these gaps is essential to maintain Kenya’s food security, safeguard rural livelihoods, and advance national development goals under the Kenya Climate Smart Agriculture Strategy 2022–2032 and Vision 2030. Data from KNBS (2025) indicate that Kenyan smallholder households contribute approximately 75–80% of national maize output, with the majority cultivating holdings of less than two hectares and mean yields reported at 1.5–1.8 tonnes per hectare (De Groote et al., 2020; Njeru et al., 2023). text: Kenya’s 4.5 million farming households—a gap this research seeks to address. 2 Purpose of the scoping literature review This scoping review aimed to systematically map and synthesize the empirical evidence base on climate change impacts and adaptation among smallholder maize producers in Low and Medium Income Countries (LMICs). Despite growing recognition of climate threats to smallholder maize systems, the existing literature remains fragmented across geographic contexts, outcome measures, and methodological approaches. This review sought to assess the extent, nature, and quality of empirical research in this domain, identify dominant themes and research gaps, and provide evidence-based recommendations for future research, policy, and practice. By clarifying what has been studied, where research is concentrated, which outcomes have received attention, and what critical evidence gaps persist, this review contributes to building a more coherent knowledge base for supporting climate-resilient smallholder maize production systems. 2.1 Study rationale and objectives Climate change poses existential threats to smallholder maize producers in LMICs, yet the empirical evidence base documenting these impacts and evaluating adaptation responses remains methodologically limited, geographically uneven, and thematically skewed. While adaptation strategies

title: fclim 8 1704769 (pp. 4-5)
text: threats to smallholder maize producers in LMICs, yet the empirical evidence base documenting these impacts and evaluating adaptation responses remains methodologically limited, geographically uneven, and thematically skewed. While adaptation strategies have been extensively documented, far less attention has been paid to measuring actual climate impacts on Gachathi and Nzengya 10.3389/fclim.2026.1704769 Frontiers in Climate 05 frontiersin.org productivity and livelihoods, understanding farmers’ perceptions and awareness of climate risks, or evaluating the effectiveness and scalability of adaptation interventions across diverse contexts. Furthermore, as is evidenced in this review, research is heavily concentrated in a few countries (notably Ethiopia, Ghana, and Zimbabwe), leaving other vulnerable regions underrepresented. Methodologically, the dominance of cross-sectional designs limits understanding of temporal dynamics, while the absence of mediating and moderating variables obscures the pathways and conditions through which adaptation occurs. This scoping review addresses these limitations by systemati cally examining peer-reviewed empirical studies published between 2008 and 2025. The review identifies dominant research themes, assesses the geographic and methodological distribution of evi dence, evaluates theoretical frameworks employed, and highlights critical gaps in knowledge. By synthesizing findings across adapta tion strategies, farmer perceptions, and climate awareness, this study provides a comprehensive assessment of the current state of knowledge and charts directions for advancing both scholarship and practice in support of climate-resilient smallholder maize systems. The general Objective of the scoping review was to map and synthesize the empirical evidence on climate change impacts, adaptation strategies, and farmer responses among smallholder maize producers in Sub-Saharan Africa, while identifying key knowledge gaps that constrain the development of effective, equitable, and sustainable pathways to resilience. The specific objectives were as follows: 1. To examine the scope, geographic distribution, methodological approaches, and theoretical frameworks used in empirical studies on climate change impacts and adaptation among smallholder maize producers in low- and middle-income countries between 2008 and 2025. 2. To assess how smallholder maize farmers perceive and understand climate change risks, and how these perceptions influence their adaptive behaviors and responses. 3. To document and

What methodological, theoretical, geographic, and contextual gaps exist in empirical literature, and what are their implications for research, policy, and practice? 3 Methodology This scoping review mapped and synthesized empirical evidence on climate change adaptation among smallholder maize farmers in Low- and Medium-Income Countries. A scoping review design was selected because it facilitates comprehensive examination of the nature, scope, and breadth of research activities within multidisciplinary fields, accommodating diverse methodologies, theoretical perspectives, and outcome measures ( Arksey and O'Malley, 2005 ; Levac et al., 2010). This approach was particularly appropriate given that literature on smallholder maize adaptation spans agricultural economics, environmental sciences, and rural sociology. The review followed the PRISMA Extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) framework (Tricco et al., 2018 ; Page et al., 2021 ), which provides structured guidance through clearly defined

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 5)
text: rural sociology. The review followed the PRISMA Extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) framework (Tricco et al., 2018 ; Page et al., 2021 ), which provides structured guidance through clearly defined stages of identification, screening, eligibility, and inclusion, enhancing methodological rigor, transparency, and reproducibility. A scoping search was conducted for peer-reviewed empirical studies published between 2008 and 2025, capturing climate adaptation trends following key policy milestones including the 2015 Paris Agreement and Africa’s 2022 Malabo Declaration on climate-resilient agriculture ( African Union Commission, 2015a, 2015b). 3.1 Search strategy A comprehensive literature search across multiple databases yielded a total of 120 records before deduplication. Following title/abstract screening and full-text eligibility checks, 38 studies were ultimately included in the scoping review. Notably, the vast majority of these 38 articles were found via mainstream indexing databases (Scopus and Web of Science). In fact, over 30 of the included studies were originally identified through Scopus, and a very high overlap with Web of Science (≈25–30 studies) was observed, reflecting the comprehensive indexing of relevant journals in these two databases. Focusing on the studies obtained from Scopus and Web of Science (which overlap with a considerable number of articles included), several notable patterns in terms of study context and content: • Countries of study: the Scopus/WoS-sourced studies spanned a range of low- and middle-income countries, with a heavy focus on Sub-Saharan Africa. This reflects a concentration of research on climate adaptation in these countries. Other countries like South Africa, Nigeria, Lesotho, Kenya, and Tanzania also featured, though in fewer studies (see Figure 1 below). This geographic emphasis aligns with the broader trend of climate-change impact studies clustering in certain African nations with high maize-dependence. • Common keywords/topics: across the Scopus/WoS studies, certain themes and variables recurred frequently. Many papers examined aspects of “climate change adaptation strategies” (e.g., adoption of drought-tolerant maize, soil and water conservation, livelihood diversification) as the primary outcome. Correspondingly, common independent variables and descriptors included terms related to farmers’ perceptions of climate risk, awareness of climate information, and adaptive capacity. Socio-economic factors were also prominent: for instance, household size, education level, gender of the household head, access to credit, extension services, farm size, and tenure security were regularly analyzed as determinants of adaptation or food security outcomes. A comprehensive search strategy was employed to ensure inclusivity and reduce selection bias. Relevant peer-reviewed literature was retrieved from five reputable databases: Scopus, Web of Science, Science Direct, Springer Link, and Google Scholar. A standardized Boolean search string was applied uniformly across all platforms: (“climate change” OR “climatic variability”) AND (“smallholder” OR “small-scale farmer”) AND (“maize” OR “corn”) AND (“adaptation” OR “resilience”). Database-specific search strategies were employed to account for variations in indexing, retrieval algorithms, and syntax, as highlighted in comparative studies of bibliographic databases (Zhu and Liu, 2020, for Scopus vs. WoS; Gusenbauer and Haddaway, 2020, for unique features on Google Scholar). While core search terms remained consistent across platforms to ensure conceptual alignment, queries were tailored to the capabilities of each database. The use of these databases ensured the search covered potential gaps in coverage for interdisciplinary or emerging topics without eligibility or access constraints tied to institutional registration and publisher agreements, which can limit broad, transparent retrieval of non-subscription content. This standardized approach maintained consistent inclusion parameters across all databases: peer-reviewed articles published between 2008 and 2025, written in English, and focusing on small holder maize production in low- and middle-income countries with emphasis on Sub-Saharan Africa. Searches were restricted to English-language publications for consistency, as English is the dominant language of scholarly communication in agricultural sci ences (Peters et al., 2020). The same exclusion criteria were applied uniformly—excluding qualitative-only studies, reviews, commen

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 7)
text: for consistency, as English is the dominant language of scholarly communication in agricultural sci ences (Peters et al., 2020). The same exclusion criteria were applied uniformly—excluding qualitative-only studies, reviews, commen taries, studies with ambiguous outcome measures, and those lack ing inferential statistics. This approach minimized database-specific bias and ensured comprehensive capture of relevant empirical evi dence across indexing platforms, enhancing reproducibility and transparency as recommended by PRISMA-ScR guidelines ( Munn et al., 2022 ). Zotero reference management software was used to identify and remove duplicates, yielding 86 unique studies that underwent title and abstract screening for eligibility. The rationale for this systematic approach lies in the necessity for transparency and reproducibility when undertaking literature mapping, with adherence to PRISMAScR ensuring consideration of all potentially relevant studies while avoiding subjective bias in article inclusion (Pham et al., 2014; Peters et al., 2015). 3.2 Document selection and eligibility criteria After removing duplicate records, 86 studies were screened for relevance using predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. The screening process was conducted by two independent reviewers to minimize bias and enhance reliability. Any discrepancies

text: records, 86 studies were screened for relevance using predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. The screening process was conducted by two independent reviewers to minimize bias and enhance reliability. Any discrepancies that emerged during the selection process were discussed and resolved through consensus to ensure objectivity and methodological rigor. Following this process, 38 empirical studies focusing on smallholder maize production and climate adaptation were retained for detailed review and synthesis. The justification for these selection criteria lies in the need to ensure empirical robustness and analytical comparability across included studies. By prioritizing quantitative and mixed-methods designs, the review captured statistically measurable relationships between climate variables, adaptation strategies, and productivity outcomes—critical dimensions for developing actionable insights and informing policy recommendations (Bozzola et al., 2021) (Table 1). 3.3 PRISMA flow diagram The PRISMA Flow Diagram illustrates the study selection process, showing the number of records identified, screened, excluded, and retained at each stage. This visual representation enhances transparency and aligns with PRISMA-ScR recommendations for documenting evidence synthesis (Page et al., 2021). 3.4 Data extraction and coding To ensure methodological rigor, a comprehensive codebook

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 7)
text: This visual representation enhances transparency and aligns with PRISMA-ScR recommendations for documenting evidence synthesis (Page et al., 2021). 3.4 Data extraction and coding To ensure methodological rigor, a comprehensive codebook was developed to standardize data extraction across included studies. This encompassed key domains including: dependent-variable themes such as climate change adaptation strategies, perceptions of climate risk, and awareness of climate impacts; independent-variable categories encompassing climatic factors (e.g., rainfall variability and temperature shifts), farm characteristics (e.g., landholding size and input intensity), socioeconomic factors (e.g., household income and education level), and institutional support mechanisms (e.g., extension services and credit access); methodological items such as research questions/hypotheses, mediation and moderation tests, theoretical frameworks, research design, sampling techniques, data-collection tools, and reliability/validity checks; and additional metadata including country/county context, target population, inferential analyses, key findings, limitations, assumptions, and calls for further research. A pilot coding phase was implemented to verify the codebook’s clarity and consistency, involving random selection of 15% of the included studies for initial coding (Saldaña, 2021). Two independent coders applied the codebook, with discrepancies resolved through iterative refinement of definitions until

text: consistency, involving random selection of 15% of the included studies for initial coding (Saldaña, 2021). Two independent coders applied the codebook, with discrepancies resolved through iterative refinement of definitions until inter-coder reliability thresholds were achieved (Table 2). Thematic synthesis followed, wherein patterns within each dependent-variable theme were identified via thematic analysis. Coded entries were aggregated into thematic matrices organized by studies and subthemes, enabling calculation of subtheme frequencies and their co-occurrence with independent variables. This culminated in generation of thematic maps to visualize interconnections among adaptation strategies, perceptions, awareness, and food-security outcomes. Studies were further classified by methodological design (quantitative, qualitative, or mixed methods) and geographic context to support subgroup analyses. Contextual metadata, including country, county, target population characteristics, and sampling tech niques, were recorded, with summary tables compiled to display study counts and salient methodological features (Pham et al., 2014; Peters et al., 2015). 3.5 Data analysis and thematic synthesis A thematic synthesis approach was employed to aggregate evidence across studies and identify dominant patterns, in line with guidance by Braun and Clarke (2006) and Thomas and Harden (2008).

It also facilitates mapping of causal linkages from climatic drivers to livelihood outcomes, which aligns with the review’s objective of constructing a conceptual framework for smallholder maize adaptation (Popay et al., 2006; Dixon-Woods et al., 2005). 3.6 Conceptual mapping and integration To integrate insights holistically, a conceptual framework (Figure 2) was constructed by mapping causal pathways from climatic variables to perceptions, adaptation strategies, and food-security outcomes. This involved annotating mediating variables (e.g., income diversification) and moderating factors (e.g., extension access), with iterative refinement against qualitative subthemes and theoretical constructs until conceptual convergence was attained (Tricco et al., 2018; Page et al., 2021). The framework traces causal pathways linking climate signals such as drought frequency, rainfall variability, and temperature rise to farmers’ risk perceptions, adaptation decisions, and livelihood outcomes. It integrates both direct and indirect relationships, recognizing that impacts of climate variability are mediated by socioeconomic and institutional factors, including income diversification, access to credit, extension services, and education. These mediating and moderating factors help explain heterogeneity in adaptive responses among smallholder

text: are mediated by socioeconomic and institutional factors, including income diversification, access to credit, extension services, and education. These mediating and moderating factors help explain heterogeneity in adaptive responses among smallholder maize farmers across different regions of Kenya and SubSaharan Africa. This conceptual integration aligns with the Sustainable Livelihoods Framework and Climate Resilience Theory, which together emphasize dynamic interactions between environmental shocks, household assets, and institutional support systems in shaping adaptation capacity (Scoones, 1998; Béné et al., 2012; Kabara et al., 2022; Karwani G. M. et al., 2024; Karwani M. et al., 2024b). By connecting empirical findings to these theoretical underpinnings, the framework not only clarifies how smallholders respond to climatic stressors but also identifies leverage points where policy interventions can strengthen resilience. The synthesis further highlights methodological rigor, evidence patterns, and persisting knowledge gaps across studies, providing a foundation for future research and informing adaptive policy design. title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 9)
text: reproducibility, the manuscript incorporates comprehensive documentation, including the PRISMA flow diagram for study selection, aggregated study limitations, and author-recommended avenues for future research. 4 Findings 4.1 Overview This scoping review synthesizes 40 peer-reviewed quantitative and mixed-methods studies on climate change impacts on smallholder maize farmers, revealing a complex landscape of adaptation strategies. The predominance of practices like drought-tolerant maize and agroforestry highlights efforts to enhance food security and resilience amid climatic variability. However, uneven exploration of socioeconomic outcomes and resilience mechanisms points to critical research gaps. Grounded in cross-sectional household surveys and advanced statistical methods, such as double-hurdle models and structural equation modeling, these findings illuminate the interplay of adaptation, farmer perceptions, and food security. However, they underscore the urgent need for research and policy to advance context-specific, scalable solutions for sustainable and climate-resilient maize farming systems. 4.1.1 Scope of research outcomes investigated The scoping review identified three thematic areas addressed across the 38 selected studies: adaptation strategies (70.3%, n = 26), perception (21.6%, n = 8) and awareness (8.1%, n = 3). Adaptation strategies emerged as the most frequently

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 9)
text: addressed across the 38 selected studies: adaptation strategies (70.3%, n = 26), perception (21.6%, n = 8) and awareness (8.1%, n = 3). Adaptation strategies emerged as the most frequently examined dependent variable, underscoring their central role in climate resilience within maize-based farming systems. Perception was the second most studied outcome, with research highlighting how farmers’ views—particularly of rainfall and temperature variability—strongly shape adaptation behavior. Awareness ranked third, with a smaller body of studies showing its influence on decision-making, risk management, and the adoption of sustainable practices among smallholder maize farmers (Figure 3). 4.1.1.1 Geographic distribution of included studies The geographic distribution of the 38 final included studies by country shows that the evidence base was highly concentrated in a few countries, with Ghana (n = 7) and Ethiopia (n = 7) contributing the highest number of studies, followed closely by Zimbabwe ( n = 7). Together, these three countries account for more than half of all included studies, indicating a strong regional focus on climate change research among smallholder maize producers in these contexts. South Africa contributed three studies,

Other countries such as Cameroon, Burkina Faso, Costa Rica, and Tanzania also appeared with one or two studies. Across nearly all countries, adaptation strategies dominated literature. For example, Ethiopia recorded five adaptation-focused studies, along with two perception studies, but no awareness-focused studies. Zimbabwe also showed a strong emphasis on adaptation (five studies), alongside two perception studies. Ghana exhibited the most balanced thematic coverage, with three adaptation studies, three perception studies, and one awareness study, making it the only country with representation across all three thematic areas. In contrast, Eswatini had only one awareness-focused study, with no studies on adaptation or perception. India was represented by a single perception-focused study, while Kenya, Botswana, Burkina Faso, Cameroon, DRC, Lesotho, Nigeria, South Africa, and Tanzania were represented exclusively by adaptation-focused studies (Figure 5). Overall, the results confirm that research on smallholder maize farmers has overwhelmingly prioritized adaptation strategies, while awareness and perception remain comparatively underexplored. Awareness studies were particularly rare and geographically limited, suggesting a major gap in understanding how knowledge and information influence adaptive behavior across different contexts. The studies span diverse agro-ecological contexts across SubSaharan Africa (such as Nigeria, Ghana, Ethiopia, Zimbabwe, Kenya, Botswana, Cameroon, Lesotho, South Africa) and Central America (Costa Rica, Guatemala, Honduras), underscoring the global urgency of climate adaptation. Mixed method approaches integrated structured questionnaires with focus group discussions, key informant interviews, and participatory ranking tools, providing valuable insights into social, institu tional, and behavioral aspects of adaptation. Several studies employed theoretical frameworks, including the Random Utility Maximization Theory, Protection Motivation Theory (PMT), Expected Utility Theory, Socio-Ecological Systems, and Theory of Production. Sampling designs were rigorous, with many studies using agro-ecological zoning, climate vulnerability, and maize production as key inclusion criteria. Variables frequently investigated include gender, education, household size, access to credit and extension services, market proximity, land ownership, and climate information access. 4.1.2 Countries of research Studies on climate impacts among smallholder maize producers in LMICs are highly uneven, concentrated in Ethiopia (19%, n = 5), Ghana, South Africa, and Zimbabwe (each 12%, n = 3), followed by Costa Rica, Lesotho, and Nigeria (each 8%, n = 2). Botswana, Burkina Faso, Cameroon, DRC, Kenya, and Tanzania account for 4% each (n = 1). This geographic skew enhances context-specific insights for Eastern and Southern Africa but limits generalizability across Latin America, Southeast Asia, the Sahel, and Francophone Africa. Adaptation strategy research spans 15 LMICs, with Eswatini leading at n = 5 studies and Ethiopia, Lesotho, and South Africa each contributing n = 3. Perception-focused studies emphasize West and East Africa; Ghana’s three studies across diverse districts illustrate agro-ecological and administrative heterogeneity, while one study each in Eswatini, India, and Tanzania indicates emerging attention in Southern Africa and South Asia ( Figure 6 ). These imbalances risk skewing regional evidence on farmer vulnerabilities and adapta tion uptake. Expanding coverage across additional LMICs and agroecological zones is essential to inform equitable, evidence-based policies that bolster resilience of smallholder maize systems globally and truly equitably. 4.1.3 Research questions Within 38 peer-reviewed studies of climate change impacts on smallholder maize producers, research questions concentrated on three main themes: perception, adaptation strategies, and environmental awareness. Perception-oriented inquiries dominated at 36% (n = 8), exploring how farmers recognize and interpret shifts in rainfall, temperature, and seasonality. These subjective experiences are deemed foundational to adaptation decision making. Questions on factors influencing adaptation featured in 27% ( n = 6) of studies, examining socioeconomic, institutional, and resource-access determinants of practices like drought-tolerant varieties, soil and water conservation, and livelihood diversification. Adaptation strategies as a direct focus comprised 18% ( n = 4), yielding practical insights for scaling field-tested responses. Less attention was devoted to empirical relationships and biophysical

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 11)
text: livelihood diversification. Adaptation strategies as a direct focus comprised 18% ( n = 4), yielding practical insights for scaling field-tested responses. Less attention was devoted to empirical relationships and biophysical diagnosis: 9% (n = 2) of questions addressed causal links between climate variables and yields or food security, while studies of long-term climate trends and direct impacts each accounted for 5% (n = 1). In the broader literature, 48% (n = 14) of studies prioritized factors shaping adaptation, 21% ( n = 6) assessed climate variability impacts on production, and 14% (n = 4) evaluated adaptation outcomes in resilience, food security, or income. Cost considerations appeared in only 7% (n = 2), and environmental awareness and concern emerged in just 3% (n = 1), highlighting limited focus on farmers’ knowledge and beliefs about climate risk (Figure 7). This distribution signals a behavioral and systemsoriented field that emphasizes individual perceptions and decision drivers over rigorous integration of biophysical evidence and awareness-building. Bridging this methodological gap will strengthen contextually relevant adaptation pathways. Expanding longitudinal, comparative studies and investing in climate literacy can

Expanding longitudinal, comparative studies and investing in climate literacy can deepen understanding of effective strategies and enhance sustainable farmerled innovations. 4.1.4 Theories Of the 38 studies reviewed, 26 employed theoretical frameworks, revealing substantial fragmentation in how climate change impacts among smallholder maize producers in low- and middle-income countries are conceptualized. Just under half (46%, n = 12) applied formal theory, while the majority (54%, n = 14) lacked explicit conceptual framing. Across theory-informed studies, the focus was primarily on farmer decision-making processes, vulnerability assessment, and adaptive capacity. Vulnerability theory was most frequently used (n = 10), particularly within adaptation strategies (n = 8) and perception analyses (n = 2), emphasizing sources of susceptibility to climate impacts and differential exposure shaped by socio-economic and environmental factors (Cáceres et al., 2024; Dilling et al., 2015; Ludena et al., 2015). The following points outline the specific theories and their areas of emphasis: • Economic rationality and utility maximization approaches were evident but surprisingly

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 11)
text: et al., 2015; Ludena et al., 2015). The following points outline the specific theories and their areas of emphasis: • Economic rationality and utility maximization approaches were evident but surprisingly limited. Random utility maximization (RUM), which focuses on farmers’ choice behavior under uncertainty and assumes rational decision-making to maximize expected benefits (Carleton et al., 2024; Stetter and Mennig, 2024), appeared only once (n = 1), contrary to assumptions of its dominance in agricultural economics literature. The random utility framework has proven versatile in modeling adaptation choices under climate change, incorporating both deterministic and stochastic components to explain how individuals choose among alternatives in the presence of uncertainty (Baulcomb et al., 2022; Tabbo et al., 2018). • Behavioral and psychological frameworks received minimal attention. Protection motivation theory (n = 1), which examines how threat appraisal and coping appraisal influence protective behaviors, appeared in one awareness study. Expected utility theory (n = 1), focusing on decision-making under risk ( Holden and Tilahun, 2024; Piracci et al., 2023), and socio-cognitive theory (n = 1), emphasizing learning and social influences on behavior (Truelove et

Recent advances in behavioral decision theory, particularly Rank-Dependent Utility Theory, have provided richer understanding of farmer behavior under extreme environmental stress, revealing that risk preferences shift significantly in high-stakes climate situations (Holden and Tilahun, 2024). • Systems-oriented approaches were underutilized. All other frame works including adaptive capability theory, Cultural Theory, deductive theory, expected utility theory, existence theory, Marshallian demand, neoclassical production, protection motiva tion, random utility maximization (RUM), socio-cognitive, socio ecological systems (SES), and sustainable intensification were referenced only once ( n = 1 each), underscoring a lack of theoreti cal convergence. This narrow framing constrains under standing of non-economic drivers—social norms, gendered barri ers, power dynamics, and local knowledge—limiting cross-contextual synthesis. Embedding diverse theoretical lenses across adaptation, perception, and awareness domains is essential to strengthen conceptual coherence and foster actionable, contextsensitive adaptation research. 4.1.5 Target populations In climate-change research on maize production among smallholders in low- and middle-income countries, there is a pronounced emphasis on smallholder maize farmers, who feature in 77% (n = 20) of studies. Smallholder farm households account for 12% ( n = 3), smallholder coffee farmers for 8% ( n = 2), and communal (subsistence) farmers for 4%

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 12)
text: (n = 20) of studies. Smallholder farm households account for 12% ( n = 3), smallholder coffee farmers for 8% ( n = 2), and communal (subsistence) farmers for 4% (n = 1). Adaptation strategies emerged as the dominant theme, with 23 of 26 studies on on-farm innovations focusing exclusively on smallholder maize producers. These farmers are portrayed not as passive victims of climate stress but as active experimenters of context-specific solutions, though adaptive capacity varies with access to extension services, improved seed, irrigation, and institutional support. Priority interventions consistently identified include droughttolerant maize cultivars, water-conservation practices (e.g., zai pits, mulching), decentralized advisory systems, and weather-indexed insurance. Perception research, represented by five studies, highlights the acute vulnerability of smallholders cultivating less than two hectares under rain-fed conditions, with fieldwork in Eswatini, Ethiopia’s Wolaita Zone, and Tanzania’s Kongwa District documenting recurrent droughts, erratic rainfall, and heatwaves (Figure 9). Broader analyses of all maize farmers (25%, n = 2) and mixed rural households (13%, n = 1) provide context but risk obscuring crop-specific adaptation needs. Awareness studies, largely labeling respondents as “smallholder

title: fclim 8 1704769 (p. 12)
text: all maize farmers (25%, n = 2) and mixed rural households (13%, n = 1) provide context but risk obscuring crop-specific adaptation needs. Awareness studies, largely labeling respondents as “smallholder farmers, ” reveal low awareness of climate risks and adaptation options in semi-arid Zimbabwean zones, with only one study explicitly defining its sample as “maize farmers. ” This inconsistency in population taxonomy limits cross-study synthesis, while the concentration on maize-growing smallholders, though policy-relevant, narrows the research lens and thus future research could broaden target populations incorporating mixed cropping and agro-pastoral systems (e.g., coffee–maize intercropping), diverse household typologies, and livelihood-system frameworks that capture gender and equity dimensions. This diversification can uncover synergies across farming contexts, strengthen the evidence base on adaptive potential, and inform the design of nuanced, scalable interventions. 4.1.6 Research designs Across sampled studies examining adaptation strategies, awareness, and perception of climate change among smallholder maize producers, research designs were overwhelmingly cross-sectional. Thirty-two studies (86%) employed cross-sectional surveys, four (11%) used exploratory approaches, and only one study (3%) adopted a longitudinal design. Under the adaptation strategies theme (n =

This uniform omission implies an assumption of homogeneity in climate impacts and responses across farmer populations. Hints of subgroup differences appear only in qualitative accounts, which note gender and age disparities in access to land, information, or credit, yet lack rigorous theoretical framing or statistical interaction tests. Reliance on descriptive and main-effect models limits under standing of why certain groups are more vulnerable or successful in adapting to climate stressors. Without moderation analysis, the literature cannot reveal how contextual factors—such as gender, education, extension services, social capital, or agro-ecological variation—alter the strength or direction of climate-adaptation relationships. Qualitative research should adopt comparative frameworks that explicitly explore subgroup contrasts and contextual conditioning factors. Many studies rely on behavioral or economic decision-making models without embedding them within comprehensive, interdisciplinary frameworks. For instance, Adeagbo et al. (2021) utilize socio-ecological systems theory to assess adaptation strategies among Nigerian maize farmers, yet the framework does not account for long-term behavioral change or resilience-building. Similarly, Belay et al. (2017) emphasize socioeconomic drivers of adaptation but fall short of linking these to structural inequalities or historical marginalization that may influence adaptive capacity. Moreover, studies often treat constructs like awareness and perception as static, self-reported variables, without integrating them into established psychological theories such as the Theory of Planned Behavior (Ajzen, 1991) or Protection Motivation Theory. For example, Boimah (2023) constructs awareness indices using binary indicators but does not engage with underlying psychological drivers of behavior. Martey et

This weak theoretical integration limits understanding of how cognition, motivation, and social influence jointly shape adaptation behavior. In addition, climate justice, vulnerability, and political economy perspectives remain marginal in adaptation research. While Mugisho et al. (2024) and Mukarumbwa (2022) document disparities in access to adaptation tools, these are not embedded within broader frameworks of vulnerability or environmental justice. Y et, different conceptualizations of vulnerability shape how adaptation priorities are defined and which groups are considered at risk (Becker et al., 2021). Gendered and communal dimensions of adaptation are also under-theorized. As noted by Kunene (2019), perception is often treated as an individual trait, neglecting its social and collective formation in rural communities. text: structural barriers, and long-term resilience outcomes among smallholder maize producers. 5.2 Empirical gaps Empirical studies on climate change impacts among smallholder maize farmers have generated valuable insights, but several gaps limit their depth, rigor, and policy relevance. A major issue is the heavy reliance on cross-sectional data, which captures behavior at a single point in time but fails to track adaptation across evolving climate conditions. Adeagbo et al. (2021), Belay et al. (2017), and Madamombe et al. (2023) all use cross-sectional survey designs, restricting their ability to assess long-term behavioral change or policy impacts. Another critical gap is the limited triangulation with objective climate data. Most studies rely on farmers’ self-reported perceptions of rainfall and temperature change, rarely validating these against meteorological records or productivity outcomes. For example, Teshome et al. (2021) compare perception data with climate trends but do not statistically test their alignment. Manzvera et al. (2024) examine willingness to pay for weather forecasts without assessing whether farmers’ perceptions reflect actual climatic variability. This weakens empirical confidence in reported climate–adaptation linkages. Geographic concentration further constrains generalizability. Research is

text: willingness to pay for weather forecasts without assessing whether farmers’ perceptions reflect actual climatic variability. This weakens empirical confidence in reported climate–adaptation linkages. Geographic concentration further constrains generalizability. Research is heavily focused on Ghana, Nigeria, Ethiopia, and Zimbabwe, leaving fragile zones, peri-urban areas, and conflict-prone regions underexplored (Boimah, 2023; Martey et al., 2022). This spatial bias risks masking unique vulnerability profiles and adaptation constraints in underrepresented regions. Social and institutional mediators also remain poorly captured. Few studies examine how social networks, institutional trust, or indigenous knowledge systems shape the relationship between perception and adaptation. As a result, the pathways through which information, norms, and authority influence farmer behavior remain unclear. Finally, a considerable number of studies exhibit limited analytical rigor. Madamombe et al. (2023) rely primarily on descriptive statistics, while Manzvera et al. (2024) apply contingent valuation methods without addressing selection bias or robustness checks. Sample sizes are often small, and survey biases such as recall or non-response bias are rarely discussed. Overall, empirical research must evolve toward longitudinal, multi-site, and mixed-method designs that integrate objective climate data, disaggregated social
